headline: Intel to buy back Apollo stake in Ireland factory for $14.2 billion
summary: Intel said it would spend $14.2 billion to buy back the 49% stake it had sold to Apollo Global Management in ‌its Ireland manufacturing facility, taking full ownership of the plant as its ‌finances improve and AI drives demand for its processors.  Apollo had paid $11.2 billion in 2024 to acquire the stake in a joint venture related to the plant in the town of Leixlip outside Dublin, giving the then-struggling Intel a cash infusion to fund its manufacturing expansion in Europe and the ‌United States.  Intel has since changed ⁠CEOs, with current top boss Lip-Bu Tan pursuing an aggressive restructuring to repair the company's finances, including job cuts and asset ⁠sales.

content.title: Intel to buy back Apollo stake in Ireland factory for $14.2 billion
content.summary: Intel said it would spend $14.2 billion to buy back the 49% stake it had sold to Apollo Global Management in ‌its Ireland manufacturing facility, taking full ownership of the plant as its ‌finances improve and AI drives demand for its processors.  Apollo had paid $11.2 billion in 2024 to acquire the stake in a joint venture related to the plant in the town of Leixlip outside Dublin, giving the then-struggling Intel a cash infusion to fund its manufacturing expansion in Europe and the ‌United States.  Intel has since changed ⁠CEOs, with current top boss Lip-Bu Tan pursuing an aggressive restructuring to repair the company's finances, including job cuts and asset ⁠sales.

headline: Intel to Buy Back Apollo’s Stake in Ireland Chip Factory for $14.2 Billion. Why the Stock Is Gaining.
summary: Intel  has agreed to buy back full control of its massive chip factory in Ireland through the repurchase of  Apollo Global Management’s  stake in the venture, sending its stock higher.  Intel stock gained 6.9% to $47.18 following the announcement.  In 2024, Apollo-managed funds and affiliates led an $11.2 billion investment to acquire a 49% stake in a joint venture related to the Ireland facility, dubbed Fab 34.

content.title: Intel to Buy Back Apollo’s Stake in Ireland Chip Factory for $14.2 Billion. Why the Stock Is Gaining.
content.summary: Intel  has agreed to buy back full control of its massive chip factory in Ireland through the repurchase of  Apollo Global Management’s  stake in the venture, sending its stock higher.  Intel stock gained 6.9% to $47.18 following the announcement.  In 2024, Apollo-managed funds and affiliates led an $11.2 billion investment to acquire a 49% stake in a joint venture related to the Ireland facility, dubbed Fab 34.

summary: Yahoo Finance Anchor Josh Lipton tracks today's top moving stocks and biggest market stories in this Market Minute, including Intel stocks (INTC) riding high after agreeing to repurchase 49% equity interest in a joint chip venture in Ireland from Apollo Global Management (APO).Shares of Cal-Maine Foods (CALM) are also rising after posting third quarter earnings.Stay up to date on the latest market action, minute-by-minute, with Yahoo Finance's Market Minute.Disclosure: Yahoo is a portfolio company of funds managed by affiliates of Apollo Global Management.

content.title: Intel to spend $14.2B to buy back Ireland fab stake, Cal-Maine stock pops
content.description:
Yahoo Finance Anchor Josh Lipton tracks today's top moving stocks and biggest market stories in this Market Minute, including Intel stocks (INTC) riding high after agreeing to repurchase 49% equity interest in a joint chip venture in Ireland from Apollo Global Management (APO).

Shares of Cal-Maine Foods (CALM) are also rising after posting third quarter earnings.

Stay up to date on the latest market action, minute-by-minute, with Yahoo Finance's Market Minute.

Disclosure: Yahoo is a portfolio company of funds managed by affiliates of Apollo Global Management.

headline: Intel Chip-Factory Buyback Brings Apollo a Payday
summary: Intel has agreed to repurchase the 49% stake in its Ireland-based microchip factory it sold to Apollo Global Management for $14.2 billion.  The buyback gives Intel the full benefit of revenue from the factory as AI-driven demand for advanced microchips sends pricing higher.  It also gives a big windfall to Apollo and the debt investors who backed the deal.

content.title: Intel Chip-Factory Buyback Brings Apollo a Payday
content.summary: Intel has agreed to repurchase the 49% stake in its Ireland-based microchip factory it sold to Apollo Global Management for $14.2 billion.  The buyback gives Intel the full benefit of revenue from the factory as AI-driven demand for advanced microchips sends pricing higher.  It also gives a big windfall to Apollo and the debt investors who backed the deal.

headline: Intel Reclaims Fab 34 As AI Manufacturing Stakes Rise For Investors
summary: Intel has agreed to repurchase Apollo Global Management's 49% stake in the Ireland Fab 34 joint venture. The transaction is valued at about $14.2b and returns full ownership of Fab 34 to Intel. The deal centers on a major manufacturing site that is intended to support next generation AI chips. Intel (NasdaqGS: INTC) is taking back full control of Fab 34 at a time when its shares trade around $48.03 and have returned 22.0% year to date. Over the past year, the stock has delivered a very...

headline: Exclusive-Intel looks to put millions more into SambaNova startup chaired by CEO Tan
summary: Chipmaker Intel has planned to invest another $15 million in SambaNova, a chip startup chaired by Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan, a Reuters review of corporate records showed.  The investment, subject to regulatory approval, would increase Intel's ownership of SambaNova to 9%, the review showed. ‌The backing follows another $35 million that Intel put into SambaNova in February, which, along with other financing, had boosted Intel’s stake in the startup to 8.2%, from 6.8% last ‌year.  The specifics of this deal and others, previously unreported, show how Intel continues to pursue transactions that may increase the personal fortune of Tan, a wealthy venture capitalist whom the chipmaker hired a ​year ago to turn the company around.

content.title: Exclusive-Intel looks to put millions more into SambaNova startup chaired by CEO Tan
